title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a community kitchen. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Sofia, The Stockroom Coordinator
Sofia volunteered in the community kitchen after a neighbour mentioned that donated food was sometimes wasted at the end of the week because nobody knew what was available. She expected to chop vegetables, but she was sent to the stockroom with a clipboard. [47]On her second week, she noticed that the fridge thermometer showed a safe temperature even when the milk felt noticeably warm.[/47] The discovery led to a replacement fridge and stricter checks. [48]Her experience managing a busy restaurant helped her design a rota that matched cooks, cleaners and packers to the busiest hours.[/48] Some volunteers disliked being assigned specific tasks, yet the new system reduced waiting and confusion. Sofia also introduced a shelf for ingredients that had to be used first. She says the kitchen became calmer when everyone could see the plan. The work reminded her that kindness needs systems, especially when hungry people are standing outside waiting for lunch.
texts.B:
### Liam, The Meal Delivery Cyclist
Liam began delivering meals after cycling past the church hall and seeing people queuing in heavy rain after the buses had stopped. [49]That sight, rather than any advertisement, made him walk in and ask whether the kitchen needed help.[/49] He thought deliveries would suit him because he was fast on a bike, but the route taught him to slow down. [50]He learned that some elderly recipients wanted a short conversation at the door more than they wanted the meal handed over quickly.[/50] At first, he worried about intruding, so he kept exchanges brief. Gradually, he learned who liked weather jokes and who preferred privacy. Liam carried meals in insulated bags and memorised which doorbells were broken. Once, unexpectedly, a woman rang the kitchen just to say he had noticed her new haircut. He now believes reliability is not dramatic, but it can make a lonely day feel safer for an afternoon.
texts.C:
### Noor, The Recipe Adapter
Noor helped plan menus because she had grown up cooking for a large busy family on a small budget during school holidays. She wanted the kitchen’s meals to feel generous rather than like leftovers. [51]After a lentil curry came back almost untouched from a children’s club, she rewrote the recipe with milder spices and added a yoghurt sauce on the side.[/51] The very next week, the trays were empty. Noor was careful not to make food bland for everyone; she simply offered choices whenever possible. [52]She also created clear allergen labels for every dish after realising that volunteers were answering questions from memory.[/52] That small change made parents more confident. Noor enjoyed the puzzle of using unpredictable donated ingredients without producing the same soup every single day. She says the best compliment was hearing teenagers ask for seconds before learning the vegetables had nearly been thrown away by a supermarket.
texts.D:
### Beth, The New Volunteer Trainer
Beth joined the kitchen because her father had depended on free meals during a period of unemployment when she was a child. [53]That memory made her determined that visitors should never feel as if they were receiving charity as a favour.[/53] She trained new volunteers, many of whom arrived nervous and apologetic. The hall was shared with several community groups, so timing mattered. [54]On Friday afternoons, she had to organise quiet preparation while a language class was still using the room next door.[/54] Beth kept instructions simple and repeated them without sounding irritated. [55]She made picture guides for packing meals so volunteers with limited English could follow the order without constantly asking for help.[/55] Her aim was confidence, not speed. [56]She now pairs each beginner with someone experienced, because she does not want the kitchen to depend on her explanations alone.[/56] That has made the whole team warmer and more independent.

questions.question: identified a food-storage safety problem that was not shown by the equipment reading?
questions.explanation: Sofia noticed the fridge thermometer seemed safe even though the milk felt warm.

questions.question: used experience from food service to organise people during the busiest periods?
questions.explanation: Sofia used restaurant management experience to design a rota for busy hours.

questions.question: decided to volunteer after seeing people waiting outside in bad weather?
questions.explanation: Liam joined after seeing people queuing in heavy rain outside the church hall.

questions.question: realised that some people valued brief personal contact more than speed?
questions.explanation: Liam learned that some elderly recipients wanted conversation more than a quick handover.

questions.question: altered a dish after the original version was not eaten by its intended group?
questions.explanation: Noor rewrote the lentil curry recipe after it came back almost untouched from the children’s club.

questions.question: introduced written food-safety information because volunteers had relied on memory?
questions.explanation: Noor created allergen labels after volunteers answered questions from memory.

questions.question: was influenced by a childhood memory of a relative needing similar support?
questions.explanation: Beth remembered her father depending on free meals during unemployment.

questions.question: had to keep preparation quiet because another group was using a nearby room?
questions.explanation: Beth organised quiet preparation while a language class was next door.

questions.question: created visual instructions to help volunteers who might not understand written language easily?
questions.explanation: Beth made picture guides for volunteers with limited English.

questions.question: uses a pairing system so that training does not rely only on them?
questions.explanation: Beth pairs beginners with experienced volunteers so the kitchen does not depend on her alone.
